FT_IC1.evidence: Uses Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study data with 10,574 early adolescents aged 9-14 years from September 2016 to January 2021

FT_IC2.evidence: Only generic '≤2 hours of screen time' from 24-hour movement guidelines composite; no specific smartphone or social media variable breakdown. Exclusion via FT-EC2: 'generic total screen time or STS composite with no phone/SM breakdown'

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; cross-sectional and longitudinal linear mixed models examining adherence to 24-hour movement guidelines and health outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Adolescent Health 2023

FT_EC7_reason: Does not meet full-text inclusion criteria (FT-IC2 failed); FT-EC7 evaluation not applicable
notes: Paper measures 24-hour movement behaviors including sleep, screen time, and physical activity. Screen time is composite/generic per 24-hour guidelines without smartphone or social media specification. Within-composite movement categories cannot be extracted as independent phone/SM rows.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study data with 10,335 participants at year 2 and 6,095 at year 3

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying victimization only; no separately measured smartphone or social media variable reported. Per E6: 'Cyberbullying paper also measures phone/SM separately = MET. Cyberbullying only = NOT_MET'. Paper measures cyberbullying exposure but does not independently measure phone/SM use.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; cross-sectional and prospective longitudinal design examining associations between cyberbullying victimization and substance use initiation

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Drug and Alcohol Dependence 2023

FT_EC7_reason: Does not meet full-text inclusion criteria (FT-IC2 failed); cyberbullying alone without separate phone/SM measurement excludes from review
notes: Paper focuses on cyberbullying victimization as exposure and substance use initiation as outcome. Cyberbullying is measured via digital devices but phone/SM use itself is not measured as independent variable. Exclusion per E6.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study data with 5,027 adolescents assessed before pandemic (10-13 years) and across six timepoints May 2020-March 2021

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific recreational screen time with platform breakdown: 'social media use and video gaming' separately assessed. Results explicitly state 'Higher social media use and video gaming was associated with shorter time in bed, later bedtimes, and longer sleep onset latency'

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effect models used to examine associations between self-reported screen time (social media, video gaming) and sleep outcomes (time in bed, bedtimes, sleep onset latency). Inferential statistical test with SM variable linked to non-SM outcome (sleep behavior)

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric associations reported: 'Higher social media use and video gaming was associated with shorter time in bed, later bedtimes, and longer sleep onset latency' with statistical results shown in abstract and results section

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis; mixed-effect models examining associations between screen time changes across pandemic timepoints and sleep behavior changes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Health Psychology 2023

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply because: (1) Paper reports screen time/SM as predictor of sleep outcomes (not as dependent variable in all analyses), and (2) outcomes include sleep behavior (non-SM outcome), thus not meeting FT-EC7 condition (a) where SM is DV in every analysis
notes: Paper examines longitudinal associations between recreational screen time (social media, video gaming) and sleep metrics during COVID-19 pandemic. Social media use specifically measured and independently associated with sleep outcomes.
